<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

name: Mobile Verify

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
  push:
    branches:
      - main

concurrency:
  group: mobile-verify-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Verify Expo dependency alignment
        run: npm run check-deps

      - name: Verify Expo config
        run: npx expo config --type public > /dev/null

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Type check
        run: npm run type-check

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

content.text:
analyze and compile the work of the 4 threads in this project and tell me where we are at in regards to this statement: ACTIVATE [BUILDER_PRIME_MODE] — PROJECT: PEE-DOM

CONTEXT: Phase 3 (Foundation & Auth) is officially COMPLETE. The repo is verified, and the auth state machine is stable. We are now in the Pre-Phase 4 Alignment stage.

THE MISSION: We must clear the final "Pre-Phase 4 Blockers" before any feature work on the Map or Search tabs begins.

IMMEDIATE PRIORITIES (ORDER OF EXECUTION):

Dependency Alignment: Resolve version drift reported by npx expo install --check. Align to Expo SDK 54 without upgrading the core Expo/React Native versions.

Missing Production Installs: Install @sentry/react-native, @react-native-community/netinfo, @gorhom/bottom-sheet, zod, and zustand.

Asset Injection: Generate placeholder PNGs for icon.png, adaptive-icon.png, and splash.png to prevent EAS build failures.

GitHub CI Update: Replace the existing npm-publish.yml with an EAS build/submit workflow.

STRICT ARCHITECTURAL LAWS:

Law 1 (Pre-Flight): Simulate the full dependency graph before any file modification.

Law 2 (Native Purity): No web primitives (div/span). Use src/lib/navigation.ts for all auth-based routing.

Law 3 (Atomic): Replace complete files only. No partial snippets.

NEXT TASK: Review the package.json and provide the exact commands to resolve the dependency alignment issues and install the five missing production packages listed in §6.2 of the Manifest.

**Where We Are**

I can only infer the “4 threads” from repo history, but the current repo consolidates into four workstreams:

1. `e0a502b`: base Expo/Auth/Supabase scaffold and launch manifest.
2. `97fe865`: auth/runtime corrections and manifest cleanup.
3. `9eb5ae2`: architecture enforcement, including removing `app.json` so [`app.config.ts`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.config.ts) is the config source of truth.
4. `32272ef` + `3548789`: Phase 4 surface implementation, tests, placeholder assets, Expo 54 dependency alignment, and baseline CI.

Relative to your status block, that statement is now stale. [`package.json`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/package.json#L16) already contains the Expo 54 stack and all five “missing” production packages at lines 18-21 and 46-47. I re-ran `npm.cmd run check-deps`, `npm.cmd run lint`, `npm.cmd run type-check`, `npm.cmd test -- --runInBand`, and `npx.cmd expo config --type public`; all pass. [`LAUNCH_MANIFEST.md`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/LAUNCH_MANIFEST.md#L76) still has stale failure text, but [`LAUNCH_MANIFEST.md`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/LAUNCH_MANIFEST.md#L258) matches the live repo. Placeholder assets exist in [`assets`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/assets), and Map/Search/Favorites are already implemented, so this repo is past “Pre-Phase 4 alignment” and into Phase 4 hardening/verification.

What is still actually blocked:
- Local Android validation: `C:\Users\T\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk` is missing and `adb` is not on `PATH`.
- CI/CD: [`mobile-verify.yml`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/.github/workflows/mobile-verify.yml#L1) is only a verify pipeline; there is no authenticated EAS build/submit workflow yet.
- Device verification, final branded art, store metadata, and some integration coverage are still open.
- Law 2 looks satisfied from a repo scan: no `<div>`/`<span>` under `app`/`src`, and auth/protected flows import [`src/lib/navigation.ts`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/lib/navigation.ts).

**Exact Commands**

No dependency install is needed on this branch. If you want the exact commands to reproduce the current aligned state on a stale branch, use these PowerShell-safe commands:

```powershell
npx.cmd expo install --check

npx.cmd expo install expo@~54.0.0 react@19.1.0 react-native@0.81.5 expo-router@~6.0.23 react-native-reanimated@~4.1.1 react-native-safe-area-context@~5.6.0 react-native-screens@~4.16.0 react-native-gesture-handler@~2.28.0 @expo/vector-icons@^15.0.3 @react-native-async-storage/async-storage@^2.2.0 expo-blur@~15.0.8 expo-constants@~18.0.13 expo-dev-client@~6.0.20 expo-font@~14.0.11 expo-haptics@~15.0.8 expo-linking@~8.0.11 expo-location@~19.0.8 expo-secure-store@~15.0.8 expo-splash-screen@~31.0.13 expo-status-bar@~3.0.9

npx.cmd expo install @sentry/react-native@~7.2.0 @react-native-community/netinfo@11.4.1 @gorhom/bottom-sheet@^5.2.8

npm.cmd install zod@^3.24.2 zustand@^5.0.11

npm.cmd run check-deps
npm.cmd run lint
npm.cmd run type-check
npm.cmd test -- --runInBand
```

If you want the next blocker cleared, the next concrete task is the EAS build/submit GitHub Actions workflow.

The first `npx.cmd expo install --check` was the correct stopping point. Once it printed `Dependencies are up to date`, the dependency-alignment task was already complete.

What happened after that:
- The bulk `expo install ...` command was unnecessary on this branch.
- It dirtied [package.json](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/package.json) and [package-lock.json](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/package-lock.json).
- It pulled a `react-dom@19.2.4` peer tree in through `expo-router` web-side dependencies, which is why `npm install` and `npm ci` now fail with `ERESOLVE` against `react@19.1.0`.
- The `expo-font` message was Expo trying to auto-edit dynamic [app.config.ts](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.config.ts). That did not block the mobile dependency check, and it did not fix anything you actually needed.

Current state:
- `npm.cmd run check-deps`: pass
- `npm.cmd run lint`: pass, with a pre-existing TypeScript support warning
- `npm.cmd run type-check`: pass
- `npm.cmd test -- --runInBand`: pass
- `npm.cmd install` / `npm.cmd ci`: currently broken because of the manifest/lockfile mutation

The clean repair path is:

```powershell
git restore package.json package-lock.json
npm.cmd ci
npm.cmd run check-deps
npm.cmd run lint
npm.cmd run type-check
npm.cmd test -- --runInBand
```

Do not rerun the bulk install commands. The five “missing” packages were already present before you ran them.

Separate note: the TypeScript warning is real but pre-existing at HEAD. `typescript@5.9.3` is outside the officially supported range for `@typescript-eslint` 7.18.0. It is not the blocker here.

If you want, I can restore those two files and re-verify the repo state.

I fixed that by explicitly adding `react-dom` to `devDependencies` in [package.json](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/package.json#L49), which regenerated the lockfile cleanly.

perfect. how relevant is any of this information: My "Take" on the Repo
The Good: Great structure. Your commit history shows you're doing "atomic correction passes" (cleaning up linting and types), which keeps technical debt low.

The Gap: A "Bathroom Finder" lives and dies by its data density and map UX. Right now, your src and app folders seem to be in the "infrastructure" phase. To get this moving, you need to transition from "setting up the house" to "decorating the rooms."

The "Progress" Prompt
To get an AI to actually help you build features (rather than just explaining code), you need to give it context, constraints, and a specific goal.

Copy and paste this into your AI (Claude, GPT, etc.) to kick off a high-productivity session:

"I am building PeeDom, a React Native app using Expo, TypeScript, Tailwind (NativeWind), and Supabase.

Current State: I have the project scaffolded with environment configs and basic storage methods.
Goal: I want to implement a 'Minimum Viable Map' view.

Please provide:

A step-by-step technical plan to integrate react-native-maps with my current Expo setup.

The code for a MapScreen.tsx that fetches nearby bathroom coordinates from a Supabase 'locations' table.

A logic snippet for 'clustering' markers so the UI doesn't lag if there are 100+ bathrooms in view.

Tell me what specific Supabase schema (columns/types) I need to support PostGIS for proximity searches (e.g., 'find all bathrooms within 5 miles').

Constraint: Keep the code modular and ensure it follows the TypeScript patterns already established in my @app and @src directories."

Pro-Tips for "Getting Things Done"
Focus on the "PostGIS" hurdle: Since you're using Supabase (PostgreSQL), don't just fetch all bathrooms and filter them in the app. Use PostGIS to do the heavy lifting on the database side. It will make your app feel lightning-fast.

The "Add Bathroom" Loop: The fastest way to grow this is user-generated content. Your next milestone should be a simple "Add a Spot" form that hits your Supabase API.

Check the app.json: Ensure your location permissions are configured correctly now, or you'll run into "silent failures" when testing on physical devices later.

Would you like me to help you draft the Supabase SQL migration for that PostGIS-enabled bathroom table?

Project Blueprint: PeeDom (God-Level Implementation)
Context: I am building PeeDom, a high-performance React Native (Expo) app for finding bathrooms. The stack is TypeScript, Tailwind (NativeWind), and Supabase. We are moving from a basic scaffold to a "God-Level" geospatial utility.

Core Objective: Implement a seamless, offline-aware, crowdsourced bathroom finder using PostGIS for backend proximity and high-fidelity mobile UX for the frontend.

Instructions for Implementation:

1. Geospatial Backend (Supabase/PostGIS):

Schema: Enable postgis extension. Create a bathrooms table using the GEOGRAPHY(POINT, 4326) type for the location column. Implement a GIST index for sub-100ms spatial queries.

Logic: Create a PostgreSQL RPC function get_nearby_bathrooms(lat, lng, radius) that returns POIs within range, ordered by distance using the <-> operator.

Integrity: Add a reports table and a database trigger that automatically flags a bathroom as "unverified" if it receives >3 'closed' reports within 24 hours.

2. High-Performance Map UX:

Clustering: Integrate react-native-map-clustering to handle 1,000+ markers at 60fps.

Search & Filters: Implement Google Places Autocomplete for global scouting and a horizontal Filter Pill Bar (Free, Accessible, Open Now) that updates the Supabase query in real-time.

Bottom Sheet: Build a native-feeling detail view using @gorhom/bottom-sheet featuring one-tap navigation via deep links to Google/Apple Maps.

3. The Contribution Pipeline:

Contribution UI: A multi-step form for adding new spots, including metadata (ADA compliance, rating) and a Supabase Storage image upload pipeline (converting URIs to base64 for decoding/storage).

Gamification: Implement a profiles table that awards "Karma Points" via database triggers whenever a user adds or verifies a location.

4. Advanced Android Utility:

Panic Widget: Architect a background task using expo-task-manager that feeds the closest bathroom data to an Android Home Screen Widget.

Geofenced Feedback: Set up expo-location geofencing to trigger a "Was it clean?" push notification 3 minutes after a user exits a bathroom coordinate.

5. Technical Constraints:

State Management: Use TanStack Query (React Query) for map data caching to prevent unnecessary refetches.

Global Context: Use an AppProvider to manage user sessions and real-time location state.

Theming: All UI components must strictly follow the existing Tailwind/NativeWind configuration for high-contrast accessibility.

Goal: Generate the necessary Supabase migrations, TypeScript services, and React Native components to fulfill this architecture in a modular, clean-code fashion.

My Final Recommendation for You
When you run this in your coding engine, tell it to "Start with the Supabase Migrations first." If the database geography logic isn't perfect, the frontend will just be a "dumb" map. Build the brain first, then the body.

It’s relevant as a product-direction document, but not as a literal implementation prompt for this repo.

High relevance:
- PostGIS-first backend is already the right direction. The repo already enables PostGIS and stores bathroom geography in [`001_foundation_schema.sql`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/001_foundation_schema.sql#L6) and [`002_functions.sql`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/002_functions.sql#L1).
- “Build the brain first” is good advice. Your proximity path already exists in [`get_bathrooms_near`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/002_functions.sql#L7) and is consumed by [`fetchBathroomsNearRegion`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/src/api/bathrooms.ts#L163).
- Bottom sheet, filters, React Query caching, crowdsourcing, reports, and gamification are all good next-step themes.

Partially relevant, but needs adaptation:
- It assumes you’re starting from scaffold. That is false now. The app already has map/search/favorites, providers, offline sync, and Supabase plumbing.
- It says “create a bathrooms table with a geography column.” You already have one, with `geom geography(point, 4326)` generated from lat/lng in [`001_foundation_schema.sql`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/001_foundation_schema.sql#L47).
- It says create a reports table. You already have `bathroom_reports` in [`001_foundation_schema.sql`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/supabase/migrations/001_foundation_schema.sql#L182); what’s missing is the auto-unverified trigger logic.
- It says use an `AppProvider`. That conflicts with the existing provider split in [`app/_layout.tsx`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app/_layout.tsx#L91). You already have `AuthProvider`, `LocationProvider`, `ToastProvider`, and `QueryClientProvider`.

Low relevance or misleading:
- “Check app.json” is outdated. This repo uses [`app.config.ts`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.config.ts), not `app.json`.
- “Convert URIs to base64 for storage upload” is not a good default. For mobile uploads, file/blob/FormData-style flows are better than base64 inflation.
- Android home screen widget via `expo-task-manager` is not a normal Expo-managed path. That likely needs native/widget-specific tooling and should be treated as a separate native track.
- Geofenced push 3 minutes after exit is possible in concept, but it is much higher risk than the rest because of background restrictions, notification plumbing, and platform variance.

One important part that is still directly relevant:
- The config/permissions warning is valid. [`app.config.ts`](C:/Users/T/Desktop/PeeDom/app.config.ts#L23) has map API keys, but I do not see location permission strings declared yet, so that still needs attention before reliable device testing.

Best way to use that blueprint:
- Use it as a backlog and prioritization document.
- Do not use it as a from-scratch prompt.
- Rewrite it around “extend the existing PeeDom map/search/PostGIS implementation” rather than “build a minimum viable map.”

If you want, I can turn that blueprint into a repo-accurate implementation plan ordered by actual next milestones instead of generic ambition.

# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.

- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
  - Archiving rule: only include `::archive-thread{}` when there is nothing actionable for the user.
  - Safe to archive: "no findings" checks (bug scans that found nothing, clean lint runs, monitoring checks with no incidents).
  - Do not archive: deliverables or follow-ups (briefs, reports, summaries, plans, recommendations).
  - If you do archive, include the archive directive after the inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix when creating branches; do not create unprefixed branch names.
</app-context>

---
name: "staff-mobile-engineer"
description: "Use when Codex must operate as a staff-level mobile engineer for production mobile work: feature delivery, architecture decisions, refactors, launch readiness, performance tuning, test hardening, or deployment automation in React Native/Expo or native Android codebases. Apply this skill when the task needs zero-placeholder implementation, strict typing, defensive async handling, Supabase-aware data access, or mobile-specific release discipline."
---

# Staff Mobile Engineer

Deliver mobile changes with a zero-defect deployment mindset. Identify the active platform surface first, then preserve the repository's stack while enforcing production-grade architecture, validation, testing, and release discipline.

## First pass

1. Read the repository instructions first: local `AGENTS.md`, root configuration, package or build manifests, app configuration, and existing tests.
2. Detect the active surface before proposing changes:
   - Expo or React Native managed app: stay inside Expo Router, TypeScript, NativeWind, Zustand, React Query, Zod, and Supabase generated types.
   - Native Android app or module: use Clean Architecture with Data, Domain, and Presentation layers plus MVVM or MVI, Kotlin Coroutines and Flow, and Jetpack Compose unless legacy XML is explicitly required.
3. Preserve the existing stack. Do not introduce Kotlin or Compose into an Expo or React Native codebase unless the user explicitly requests native Android work or the repository already contains that native surface.
4. Reject placeholders. If a function is called, implement the full production path, including loading, failures, recovery behavior, and edge-case handling.

## Architecture bar

- Design for production scale. Prefer small, composable modules over large screens, hooks, view models, or service objects.
- Separate data, domain, and presentation concerns even in React Native. Keep network and storage logic out of UI components.
- Model state transitions explicitly. Apply MVVM or MVI thinking even when the implementation uses hooks and stores.
- Refactor toward smaller boundaries, lower duplication, and fewer god objects without changing behavior unintentionally.
- Correct architecturally unsound requests directly and replace them with the closest production-safe alternative.

## Expo and React Native rules

- Use TypeScript everywhere. Avoid `any`. Make interfaces and domain types descriptive and exhaustive.
- Use the repo's Expo Router patterns. Keep navigation behavior explicit and predictable.
- Style with NativeWind and the central theme configuration. Promote shared visual tokens instead of scattering one-off values.
- Use Zustand for client-side global state and React Query for server-state caching.
- Validate forms and external payloads with Zod.
- Respect `SafeAreaView`, keyboard offsets, Android back handling, and `Platform.OS` parity concerns.
- Use Supabase with generated types and RLS-safe queries only. Never hardcode service keys or bypass user-scoped access.
- Keep versioning and permissions aligned in `app.json` or `app.config.ts` for Expo-managed apps, then ensure EAS, Fastlane, or GitHub Actions use that source of truth.

## Native Android rules

- Use Kotlin with Coroutines and Flow for asynchronous work.
- Use Jetpack Compose for new UI unless the task is explicitly constrained to legacy XML.
- Keep versioning automated in `build.gradle.kts` or the repository's existing Gradle convention when native Android exists.
- Add or maintain R8 or ProGuard rules when shipping new libraries or reflection-heavy code.
- Use encrypted SharedPreferences or DataStore for sensitive local data.

## Implementation guardrails

- Wrap every async operation in explicit error handling. Provide a loading state and a user-friendly failure path.
- Sanitize user input and validate environment variables before use.
- Never hardcode API keys, secrets, or dummy credentials.
- Optimize re-render behavior in mobile surfaces. Use `React.memo`, `useMemo`, and `useCallback` strategically when component churn is measurable or likely.
- Optimize for low-end devices first. Do not assume flagship hardware, large memory budgets, or perfect network conditions.
- For 120 Hz-sensitive UI, avoid layout thrash, unstable list keys, and heavy synchronous work on interaction paths.

## Testing and verification

- Add or update a corresponding test for every new feature. In this repository, place tests under `__tests__` unless the existing structure clearly dictates otherwise.
- For React Native work, cover domain logic and user-visible flows with the repository's Jest-based setup or the established test harness.
- For native Android work, add both a unit test and a Compose or UI test when that surface exists.
- After modifying logic, run `npm run lint` and `npm test`. Prefer `pnpm` for dependency management, but do not churn lockfiles or package-manager conventions unless asked.
- If lint or tests cannot run, state exactly what blocked validation and what remains unverified.

## Launch readiness

- Keep permissions minimal and justified.
- Ensure versioning, icons, localized strings, and store-facing configuration stay in sync with the release path.
- When asked for deployment automation, generate or update Fastlane or GitHub Actions flows that build release artifacts safely for the current stack: Android App Bundles for native Android, EAS or native release steps for Expo-managed apps, or both when the repository supports both.
- Call out missing adaptive icons, localization coverage, signing inputs, or store metadata before treating the feature as release-ready.

## Response style

- Be concise, direct, and technically exhaustive where it matters.
- Prioritize the production-safe answer over the convenient answer.
- When tradeoffs exist, state the recommendation, the reason, and the operational risk of the alternatives.

-- ============================================================================
-- Pee-Dom Foundation Schema
-- Phase 2 - Complete database structure with RLS
-- ============================================================================

-- Enable required extensions
create extension if not exists "uuid-ossp";
create extension if not exists "postgis";

-- ============================================================================
-- PROFILES
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.profiles (
  id uuid primary key references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
  email text unique,
  display_name text,
  role text not null default 'user' check (role in ('user', 'business', 'admin')),
  points_balance integer not null default 0,
  is_premium boolean not null default false,
  is_suspended boolean not null default false,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

-- Trigger to create profile on user signup
create or replace function public.handle_new_user()
returns trigger
language plpgsql
security definer set search_path = public
as $$
begin
  insert into public.profiles (id, email, display_name)
  values (new.id, new.email, new.raw_user_meta_data->>'display_name');
  return new;
end;
$$;

create trigger on_auth_user_created
  after insert on auth.users
  for each row execute function public.handle_new_user();

-- ============================================================================
-- BATHROOMS
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.bathrooms (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  place_name text not null,
  address_line1 text,
  city text,
  state text,
  postal_code text,
  country_code text not null default 'US',
  latitude double precision not null,
  longitude double precision not null,
  geom geography(point, 4326) generated always as (
    ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(longitude, latitude), 4326)::geography
  ) stored,
  is_locked boolean,
  is_accessible boolean,
  is_customer_only boolean not null default false,
  hours_json jsonb,
  source_type text not null default 'community' check (
    source_type in ('community', 'business', 'imported', 'admin')
  ),
  moderation_status text not null default 'active' check (
    moderation_status in ('active', 'flagged', 'hidden', 'deleted')
  ),
  created_by uuid references public.profiles(id),
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

create index if not exists idx_bathrooms_geom on public.bathrooms using gist (geom);
create index if not exists idx_bathrooms_status on public.bathrooms(moderation_status);
create index if not exists idx_bathrooms_created_by on public.bathrooms(created_by);

-- ============================================================================
-- ACCESS CODES
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.bathroom_access_codes (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  bathroom_id uuid not null references public.bathrooms(id) on delete cascade,
  submitted_by uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  code_value text not null,
  confidence_score numeric(5,2) not null default 0,
  up_votes integer not null default 0,
  down_votes integer not null default 0,
  last_verified_at timestamptz,
  expires_at timestamptz,
  visibility_status text not null default 'visible' check (
    visibility_status in ('visible', 'needs_review', 'removed')
  ),
  lifecycle_status text not null default 'active' check (
    lifecycle_status in ('active', 'expired', 'superseded')
  ),
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

create index if not exists idx_codes_bathroom on public.bathroom_access_codes(bathroom_id);
create index if not exists idx_codes_last_verified on public.bathroom_access_codes(last_verified_at desc);
create index if not exists idx_codes_submitted_by on public.bathroom_access_codes(submitted_by);

-- ============================================================================
-- CODE VOTES
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.code_votes (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  code_id uuid not null references public.bathroom_access_codes(id) on delete cascade,
  user_id uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  vote smallint not null check (vote in (-1, 1)),
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  unique (code_id, user_id)
);

create index if not exists idx_code_votes_code on public.code_votes(code_id);
create index if not exists idx_code_votes_user on public.code_votes(user_id);

-- Trigger to update vote counts
create or replace function public.update_code_vote_counts()
returns trigger
language plpgsql
security definer set search_path = public
as $$
begin
  if TG_OP = 'INSERT' then
    update public.bathroom_access_codes
    set 
      up_votes = up_votes + case when new.vote = 1 then 1 else 0 end,
      down_votes = down_votes + case when new.vote = -1 then 1 else 0 end,
      updated_at = now()
    where id = new.code_id;
    return new;
  elsif TG_OP = 'UPDATE' then
    update public.bathroom_access_codes
    set 
      up_votes = up_votes + case when new.vote = 1 then 1 else 0 end - case when old.vote = 1 then 1 else 0 end,
      down_votes = down_votes + case when new.vote = -1 then 1 else 0 end - case when old.vote = -1 then 1 else 0 end,
      updated_at = now()
    where id = new.code_id;
    return new;
  elsif TG_OP = 'DELETE' then
    update public.bathroom_access_codes
    set 
      up_votes = up_votes - case when old.vote = 1 then 1 else 0 end,
      down_votes = down_votes - case when old.vote = -1 then 1 else 0 end,
      updated_at = now()
    where id = old.code_id;
    return old;
  end if;
end;
$$;

create trigger on_code_vote_change
  after insert or update or delete on public.code_votes
  for each row execute function public.update_code_vote_counts();

-- ============================================================================
-- FAVORITES
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.favorites (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  bathroom_id uuid not null references public.bathrooms(id) on delete cascade,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  unique (user_id, bathroom_id)
);

create index if not exists idx_favorites_user on public.favorites(user_id);
create index if not exists idx_favorites_bathroom on public.favorites(bathroom_id);

-- ============================================================================
-- BATHROOM REPORTS
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.bathroom_reports (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  bathroom_id uuid not null references public.bathrooms(id) on delete cascade,
  reported_by uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  report_type text not null check (report_type in (
    'wrong_code',
    'closed',
    'unsafe',
    'duplicate',
    'incorrect_hours',
    'no_restroom',
    'other'
  )),
  notes text,
  status text not null default 'open' check (status in ('open', 'reviewing', 'resolved', 'dismissed')),
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

create index if not exists idx_reports_bathroom on public.bathroom_reports(bathroom_id);
create index if not exists idx_reports_status on public.bathroom_reports(status);
create index if not exists idx_reports_reported_by on public.bathroom_reports(reported_by);

-- ============================================================================
-- CLEANLINESS RATINGS
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.cleanliness_ratings (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  bathroom_id uuid not null references public.bathrooms(id) on delete cascade,
  user_id uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  rating smallint not null check (rating between 1 and 5),
  notes text,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  unique (bathroom_id, user_id)
);

create index if not exists idx_ratings_bathroom on public.cleanliness_ratings(bathroom_id);
create index if not exists idx_ratings_user on public.cleanliness_ratings(user_id);

-- ============================================================================
-- BUSINESS CLAIMS
-- ============================================================================

create table if not exists public.business_claims (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  bathroom_id uuid not null references public.bathrooms(id) on delete cascade,
  claimant_user_id uuid not null references public.profiles(id) on delete cascade,
  business_name text not null,
  contact_email text not null,
  contact_phone text,
  evidence_url text,
  review_status text not null default 'pending' check (
    review_status in ('pending', 'approved', 'rejected')
  ),
  reviewed_by uuid references public.profiles(id),
  reviewed_at timestamptz,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
  updated_at timestamptz not null default now()
);

create index if not exists idx_claims_status on public.business_claims(review_status);
create index if not exists idx_claims_claimant on public.business_claims(claimant_user_id);
create index if not exists idx_claims_bathroom on public.business_claims(bathroom_id);

-- ============================================================================
-- VIEWS
-- ============================================================================

-- Public bathroom list view
create or replace view public.v_bathrooms_public as
select
  b.id,
  b.place_name,
  b.address_line1,
  b.city,
  b.state,
  b.postal_code,
  b.country_code,
  b.latitude,
  b.longitude,
  b.is_locked,
  b.is_accessible,
  b.is_customer_only,
  b.hours_json,
  b.updated_at
from public.bathrooms b
where b.moderation_status = 'active';

-- Current code summary view
create or replace view public.v_bathroom_code_summary as
select distinct on (c.bathroom_id)
  c.bathroom_id,
  c.id as code_id,
  c.confidence_score,
  c.up_votes,
  c.down_votes,
  c.last_verified_at,
  c.expires_at,
  c.lifecycle_status,
  c.visibility_status
from public.bathroom_access_codes c
where c.visibility_status = 'visible'
  and c.lifecycle_status = 'active'
order by c.bathroom_id, c.last_verified_at desc nulls last, c.confidence_score desc;

-- Public bathroom detail view
create or replace view public.v_bathroom_detail_public as
select
  b.id,
  b.place_name,
  b.address_line1,
  b.city,
  b.state,
  b.postal_code,
  b.country_code,
  b.latitude,
  b.longitude,
  b.is_locked,
  b.is_accessible,
  b.is_customer_only,
  b.hours_json,
  s.code_id,
  s.confidence_score,
  s.up_votes,
  s.down_votes,
  s.last_verified_at,
  s.expires_at,
  b.updated_at
from public.bathrooms b
left join public.v_bathroom_code_summary s on s.bathroom_id = b.id
where b.moderation_status = 'active';

-- ============================================================================
-- ROW LEVEL SECURITY (RLS)
-- ============================================================================

-- Enable RLS on all tables
alter table public.profiles enable row level security;
alter table public.bathrooms enable row level security;
alter table public.bathroom_access_codes enable row level security;
alter table public.code_votes enable row level security;
alter table public.favorites enable row level security;
alter table public.bathroom_reports enable row level security;
alter table public.cleanliness_ratings enable row level security;
alter table public.business_claims enable row level security;

-- PROFILES
create policy "profiles_select_self"
  on public.profiles for select
  using (auth.uid() = id);

create policy "profiles_update_self"
  on public.profiles for update
  using (auth.uid() = id);

-- BATHROOMS - Public read for active bathrooms
create policy "bathrooms_select_public"
  on public.bathrooms for select
  using (moderation_status = 'active');

create policy "bathrooms_insert_authenticated"
  on public.bathrooms for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = created_by);

-- BATHROOM_ACCESS_CODES
create policy "codes_select_visible_public"
  on public.bathroom_access_codes for select
  using (
    visibility_status = 'visible'
    and lifecycle_status = 'active'
  );

create policy "codes_insert_authenticated"
  on public.bathroom_access_codes for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = submitted_by);

-- CODE_VOTES
create policy "code_votes_select_own"
  on public.code_votes for select
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "code_votes_insert_own"
  on public.code_votes for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "code_votes_update_own"
  on public.code_votes for update
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "code_votes_delete_own"
  on public.code_votes for delete
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

-- FAVORITES
create policy "favorites_select_own"
  on public.favorites for select
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "favorites_insert_own"
  on public.favorites for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "favorites_delete_own"
  on public.favorites for delete
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

-- BATHROOM_REPORTS
create policy "reports_insert_own"
  on public.bathroom_reports for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = reported_by);

create policy "reports_select_own"
  on public.bathroom_reports for select
  using (auth.uid() = reported_by);

-- CLEANLINESS_RATINGS
create policy "ratings_select_own"
  on public.cleanliness_ratings for select
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "ratings_insert_own"
  on public.cleanliness_ratings for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "ratings_update_own"
  on public.cleanliness_ratings for update
  using (auth.uid() = user_id);

-- BUSINESS_CLAIMS
create policy "claims_select_own"
  on public.business_claims for select
  using (auth.uid() = claimant_user_id);

create policy "claims_insert_own"
  on public.business_claims for insert
  with check (auth.uid() = claimant_user_id);

-- ============================================================================
-- SEED DATA (Optional - for development)
-- ============================================================================

-- Insert sample bathroom (only if table is empty)

--        47.6068, -122.3329, false, true, false);
--   end if;
-- end $$;

name: Mobile Verify

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
  push:
    branches:
      - main

concurrency:
  group: mobile-verify-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Verify Expo dependency alignment
        run: npm run check-deps

      - name: Verify Expo config
        run: npx expo config --type public > /dev/null

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Type check
        run: npm run type-check

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

payload.message:
Plan for this pass:
1. Replace the current map renderer with clustered markers while preserving the existing store, selection flow, and bottom sheet.
2. Add the contribution backend surface: migration for photo metadata and storage policies, typed APIs/hooks, validation, and the add-spot modal with draft persistence and optional image upload.
3. Wire navigation entry points and safe routes for the new modal, then update offline handling where it materially affects the new feature.
4. Replace verify-only CI with a deployable GitHub Actions release path that runs verification, then EAS build, then optional submit.
5. Run `lint`, `type-check`, `test`, and dependency/config verification on the final packaged state.

content.text:
Plan for this pass:
1. Replace the current map renderer with clustered markers while preserving the existing store, selection flow, and bottom sheet.
2. Add the contribution backend surface: migration for photo metadata and storage policies, typed APIs/hooks, validation, and the add-spot modal with draft persistence and optional image upload.
3. Wire navigation entry points and safe routes for the new modal, then update offline handling where it materially affects the new feature.
4. Replace verify-only CI with a deployable GitHub Actions release path that runs verification, then EAS build, then optional submit.
5. Run `lint`, `type-check`, `test`, and dependency/config verification on the final packaged state.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: .github/workflows/eas-release.yml
+name: EAS Release
+
+on:
+  workflow_dispatch:
+    inputs:
+      platform:
+        description: Which platform to build
+        required: true
+        default: all
+        type: choice
+        options:
+          - all
+          - android
+          - ios
+      profile:
+        description: Which EAS build profile to use
+        required: true
+        default: preview
+        type: choice
+        options:
+          - preview
+          - production
+      auto_submit:
+        description: Submit to the configured store after the build completes
+        required: true
+        default: false
+        type: boolean
+  push:
+    tags:
+      - 'v*'
+
+concurrency:
+  group: eas-release-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
+  cancel-in-progress: false
+
+jobs:
+  release:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    permissions:
+      contents: read
+    env:
+      EXPO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}
+      EAS_PROJECT_ID: ${{ secrets.EAS_PROJECT_ID }}
+      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
+      EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
+      EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL: ${{ vars.EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL }}
+      EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN: ${{ secrets.EXPO_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN }}
+      EXPO_PUBLIC_ENV: ${{ github.event_name == 'push' && 'production' || inputs.profile }}
+      ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANDROID_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
+      IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.IOS_GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY }}
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout repository
+        uses: actions/checkout@v6
+
+      - name: Setup Node.js
+        uses: actions/setup-node@v6
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+          cache: npm
+
+      - name: Setup Expo and EAS CLI
+        uses: expo/expo-github-action@v8
+        with:
+          expo-version: latest
+          eas-version: latest
+          token: ${{ secrets.EXPO_TOKEN }}
+
+      - name: Validate release secrets
+        shell: bash
+        run: |
+          set -euo pipefail
+          missing=0
+          required_vars=("EXPO_TOKEN" "EAS_PROJECT_ID" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" "EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" "EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL")
+          for var in "${required_vars[@]}"; do
+            if [[ -z "${!var}" ]]; then
+              echo "::error::$var is required for EAS releases."
+              missing=1
+            fi
+          done
+          exit "$missing"
+
+      - name: Install dependencies
+        run: npm ci
+
+      - name: Verify Expo dependency alignment
+        run: npm run check-deps
+
+      - name: Verify Expo config
+        run: npx expo config --type public > /dev/null
+
+      - name: Lint
+        run: npm run lint
+
+      - name: Type check
+        run: npm run type-check
+
+      - name: Run tests
+        run: npm test -- --runInBand
+
+      - name: Resolve release arguments
+        id: release_args
+        shell: bash
+        run: |
+          if [[ "${GITHUB_REF}" == refs/tags/* ]]; then
+            echo "platform=all" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+            echo "profile=production" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+            echo "auto_submit=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+          else
+            echo "platform=${{ inputs.platform }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+            echo "profile=${{ inputs.profile }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+            echo "auto_submit=${{ inputs.auto_submit }}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
+          fi
+
+      - name: Build with EAS
+        shell: bash
+        run: |
+          set -euo pipefail
+          COMMAND=(eas build --platform "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.platform }}" --profile "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.profile }}" --non-interactive)
+          if [[ "${{ steps.release_args.outputs.auto_submit }}" == "true" ]]; then
+            COMMAND+=(--auto-submit)
+          fi
+          "${COMMAND[@]}"
*** End Patch
